93	```

102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}

125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were

12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}

118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}

153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}

172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}

285	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then

287	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
288	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
289	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
290	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
291	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
292	  \[
293	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
294	  \]
295	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
296	\end{theorem}
297	
298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
299	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
300	% be the quotient:
301	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
302	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
303	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
304	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
305	% Ex. 1.16b)
306	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
307	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
308	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
309	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
310	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
311	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
312	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
313	% \(i\) we get the result."
314	\begin{proof}
315	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
316	  \leanok
317	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
318	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
319	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
320	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
321	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
322	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
323	  short exact sequence
324	  \[
325	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
326	  \]
327	  in the sheaf category.
328	
329	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
330	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
331	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
332	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
333	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
334	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
335	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
336	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
337	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
338	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
339	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
340	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
341	
342	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
343	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
344	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
345	  exact sequence on global sections
346	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
347	  \[
348	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
349	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
350	  \]
351	
352	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
353	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
354	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
355	  positive degree:
356	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
357	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
358	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
359	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
360	  \[
361	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
362	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
363	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
364	  \]
365	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
366	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
367	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
368	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
369	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
370	
371	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
372	  exact sequence yields
373	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
374	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
375	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
376	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
377	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
378	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
379	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
380	
381	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
382	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
383	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
384	  % already provided by chapter
385	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
386	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
387	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
388	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
389	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
390	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
391	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
392	  % LOC apiece.
393	\end{proof}

395	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
396	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
397	
398	\begin{lemma}
399	
400	\leanok
401	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
402	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
403	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
404	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
405	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
406	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
407	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
408	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
409	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
410	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
411	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
412	  % X\) is the inclusion."
413	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
414	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
415	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
416	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
417	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
418	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
419	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
420	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
421	\end{lemma}
422	
423	\begin{proof}
424	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
425	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
426	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
427	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
428	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
429	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
430	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
431	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
432	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
433	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
434	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
435	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
436	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
437	\end{proof}

439	\begin{lemma}
440	
441	\leanok
442	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
443	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
445	
446	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
447	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
448	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
449	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
450	
451	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
452	  no external source pinned).
453	\end{lemma}
454	
455	\begin{proof}
456	
457	
458	\leanok
459	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
460	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
461	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
462	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
463	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
464	\end{proof}

466	\begin{lemma}
467	
468	\leanok

472	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
473	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
474	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
475	
476	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
477	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
478	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
479	  \[
480	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
481	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
482	  \]
483	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
484	
485	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
486	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
487	  above).
488	\end{lemma}
489	
490	\begin{proof}
491	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward, lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible, lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
492	  \leanok

495	  isomorphic to the pushforward

509	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
510	
511	\begin{theorem}
512	
513	\leanok
514	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
515	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}

517	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
518

520	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
521	  \[
522	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
523	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
524	  \]
525	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
526	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
527	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
528	
529	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
530	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
531	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
532	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
533	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
534	  above.
535	\end{theorem}
536	
537	\begin{proof}
538	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
539	  \leanok
540	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
541	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
542	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
543	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
544	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
545	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
546	  \[
547	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
548	  \]

550	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
551	\end{proof}

236	/-- **Generic LES vanishing lemma**: in an abelian category with enough Ext,
237	if `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` is short exact with `S.X₂` injective and the
238	post-composition `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` is surjective, then
239	`Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`.
240	
241	This is the structural skeleton of the Hartshorne III.2.5 argument
242	specialised at degree 1: the long exact sequence
243	`Ext X S.X₂ 0 → Ext X S.X₃ 0 → Ext X S.X₁ 1 → Ext X S.X₂ 1`
244	collapses because the rightmost term vanishes (injectivity of `S.X₂`)
245	and the leftmost map is surjective by the `Hom`-level hypothesis. The
246	proof composes:
247	- `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (injective ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1 = 0`).
248	- `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` (any `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1` lifts
249	  to some `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism).
250	- `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` (identifies `Ext X S.X₃ 0` with `Hom(X, S.X₃)`).
251	- `comp_extClass_assoc` (the LES "complex" identity used to discharge
252	  `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp extClass = 0`).

255	off the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` once the
256	`Hom(X, I) → Hom(X, G)`-surjectivity input (Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b))

260	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
261	    (hsurj : Function.Surjective (fun (f : X ⟶ S.X₂) => f ≫ S.g))
262	    (x₁ : Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ 1) : x₁ = 0 := by
263	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ 1) :=
264	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 1 le_rfl _
265	  have hker : x₁.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega) = 0 :=
266	    Subsingleton.elim _ _
267	  obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
268	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker
269	      (n₀ := 0) (show 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)
270	  obtain ⟨y, hy⟩ := hsurj (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃)
271	  simp only at hy
272	  have hx3eq : Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃) = x₃ := by
273	    rw [← Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀_symm_apply, AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
274	  have hx3_factored :
275	      x₃ = (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y).comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g)
276	        (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) := by
277	    rw [Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, hy, hx3eq]
278	  rw [← hx₃, hx3_factored]
279	  rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y) (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g) hS.extClass
280	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega)
281	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)]
282	  have hSg_extClass_zero :
283	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp hS.extClass (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega) = 0 := by

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
  and dense, so the constant presheaf
  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
  flasque.

  % NOTE (iter-197 lean-vs-blueprint-checker M-1): The "restriction map
  % is the identity on A" sentence above describes the underlying
  % mathematical fact correctly, but presents the non-empty branch as
  % immediate. In Lean (snapshot `b80f227`) this branch is non-trivial:
  % the constant sheaf
  % `(constantSheaf J D).obj A` is built as the sheafification of the
  % literal constant presheaf, and the sheafification unit
  % \(\eta\colon (\mathrm{Functor.const}\;C^{\mathrm{op}})\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\,A
  % \;\to\;((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;D)\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\,A)\,.\,\mathrm{val}\)
  % is iso on every non-empty open of an irreducible space, but Mathlib
  % does NOT ship this lemma at the present generality.  The Lean file
  % `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean` (around L150-154 of
  % `Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`) documents the gap; the
  % empty branch is closed axiom-clean, the non-empty branch carries a
  % typed sorry pending one of two closure routes:
  %
  % Route A (preferred — Mathlib upstreaming candidate, smallest project
  % delta). Provide `Full` and `Faithful` instances for the functor

\begin{lemma}[Injective objects of $\mathrm{Sheaf}(X, \Module \bar k)$ are flasque]
  \label{lem:isFlasque_injective}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Lemma~2.4 (read from
  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf — the project's local
  % .md transcript does not extend to this passage, so no verbatim quote
  % is pasted in this block per the iter-197 directive; the citation
  % pointer is recorded for downstream retrieval).
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Lemma~2.4.}

  Let \(I\) be an injective object of
  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
  (\Module \bar k)\). Then \(I\) is flasque in the sense of
  \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}: for every inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) of
  opens of \(X\), the restriction map
  \(I(U) \to I(V)\) is surjective on underlying \(\bar k\)-modules.

  The classical argument (Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4) uses the
  extension-by-zero \(j_!\) functor: for an open inclusion
  \(j_V \colon V \hookrightarrow X\), one has the short exact sequence
  \(0 \to j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to j_{U!}\mathcal O_U \to
  j_{U!}\mathcal O_U / j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to 0\), and injectivity of
  \(I\) makes the induced
  \(\mathrm{Hom}(j_{U!}\mathcal O_U, I) \to
    \mathrm{Hom}(j_{V!}\mathcal O_V, I)\) surjective. Translating
  through the \(j_{(-)!} \dashv j_{(-)}^*\) adjunction (or the
  equivalent presheaf form for the constant ring sheaf \(\bar k\))
  identifies the two Hom-sets with \(I(U)\) and \(I(V)\) respectively,
  delivering the surjective restriction map.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
  an isomorphism of sheaves.

  In the Lean formalisation this argument is split into two pieces.
  The \emph{outer} step is the presheaf-level equality
  \[
    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A \;=\;
      \bigl(i_*\;\bigr)
      \bigl(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A\bigr)
  \]
  obtained from Mathlib's \texttt{skyscraperPresheaf\_eq\_pushforward}
  via \texttt{ObjectProperty.FullSubcategory.ext} (axiom-clean, no
  Mathlib gap). The \emph{inner} step is the iso
  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A \cong
    (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit})\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A\)
  on the one-point space \(\PUnit\); this is the actual blocking
  technical step and is isolated as
  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit} below. Composing
  the two pieces (with the inner iso unpacked via
  \texttt{Classical.choice} from its current \(\mathtt{Nonempty}\)
  wrapper) gives the lemma.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Inner iso: skyscraperSheaf vs.\ constantSheaf on $\PUnit$]
  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
  \textit{Project-bespoke sub-lemma} (Lean-substrate technical step
  for \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}; no separate external
  source — the underlying mathematical content is the one-point case
  of Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.17 quoted in
  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}).

  Let \(\PUnit\) denote the one-point topological space, let
  \(J_{\PUnit}\) denote the Grothendieck topology of opens on
  \(\PUnit\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module. Then there is an
  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(\PUnit\)
  \[
    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A \;\cong\;
      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k)\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A.
  \]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
  The space \(\PUnit\) has exactly two opens, \(\bot\) (empty) and
  \(\top\) (the whole space, which contains \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\)).
  We build the iso pointwise on these two opens and check naturality
  on the unique non-identity inclusion \(\bot \le \top\).

  \emph{Forward map.} Apply Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{constantSheafAdj}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k\;
   h_{T\top}\,.\,\mathtt{homEquiv}\,.\,\mathtt{symm}\) to the natural
  identification
  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A\,.\,\mathrm{val}\,.
    \,\mathrm{obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;\top) = A\),
  which holds by \(\mathtt{simp}\;[\mathtt{skyscraperPresheaf}]\) since
  \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit} \in \top\).

  \emph{Inverse map.} Build pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\):
  \begin{itemize}
    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\bot\): both sheaves give the terminal
      object in \(\Module \bar k\) (the constant sheaf is the
      sheafification of the constant presheaf, whose value at the
      empty open is the zero module after sheafification; the
      skyscraper sheaf at the empty open is by construction
      \(0\)). Use \(\mathtt{IsTerminal.uniqueUpToIso}\) to identify
      them;
    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\): use the constantSheaf-unit-iso on
      \(\PUnit\), which is true since the \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\)
      functor is fully faithful on \(\PUnit\) (this fully-faithful
      instance is the Mathlib gap described below in the route
      choice).
  \end{itemize}

  \emph{Naturality} on the unique non-identity inclusion
  \(\bot \le \top\): both sides land in the terminal object at
  \(\bot\), so commutativity is automatic by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton}\)
  of the terminal object.

  \paragraph{Mathlib gap (route choice).} The inverse map's
  \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\) component needs that the
  \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\) functor on \(\PUnit\) is fully faithful.
  Two routes close the gap; both are tracked for the iter-197+
  prover:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Route A} (preferred — Mathlib upstreaming
      candidate): provide instances
      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
      \(.\) \texttt{Full} and
      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
      \(.\) \texttt{Faithful}. Likely the smallest project-side
      change once a Mathlib PR for the analogous fact for the
      one-point space is accepted; the inverse map then follows by
      \texttt{Functor.preimage} of the forward map.
    \item \emph{Route B} (fallback — no Mathlib upstream): directly
      construct the iso pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\) as
      above, using \(\mathtt{Sheaf.IsTerminal}\) at \(\bot\) and an
      explicit value extraction at \(\top\) (the constant sheaf at
      the unique non-empty open of an irreducible space is the
      identity on \(A\) after sheafification — same fact as
      \cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible} for the non-empty
      branch, used here only at the one-point space). Estimated
      LOC: ~50-80 standalone.
  \end{itemize}
\end{proof}

## Summary

findings (M-1, M-2, M-3) and the 3 major findings (J-1, J-2, J-3) of
the iter-196 lean-vs-blueprint-checker report. The edits add precise

constantSheaf sheafification-unit-iso on irreducible spaces, and the
`j_!` extension-by-zero functor in the sheaf-of-modules generality),
isolate the actual Lean blocking step (the inner iso

existing `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers were touched; the chapter
remains valid LaTeX (begin/end environments balanced;
`leanblueprint web` reports no errors on this chapter file — the build
error in the trace is in the sibling `RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex` L657 and
is unrelated to this directive).

## Must-fix items addressed

- **M-1** — Added a `% NOTE:` block to the proof of

  in the non-empty branch (the sheafification-unit-iso on irreducible
  spaces is not in Mathlib `b80f227`). The note describes both
  closure routes:
  - Route A (preferred, Mathlib upstreaming): `Full` + `Faithful`
    instances for `(constantSheaf J D)` on `IrreducibleSpace X`.
  - Route B (fallback, no upstream): build alternate sheaf `P'` with
    `P'(U) = A` for `U ≠ ⊥`, `P'(⊥) = 0`; exhibit iso with
    `(constantSheaf J D).obj A`.
  Cross-referenced to the Lean comment at H1Vanishing.lean:150-154
  that documents the gap on the Lean side.

- **M-2** — Updated the prose of `lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`
  to drop the misleading "naturally isomorphic" claim (changed to
  the weaker "isomorphic"), and added a `% NOTE:` documenting that
  the Lean signature is `Nonempty (iso)`, forced by `Classical.choice`

  inner iso is constructed explicitly, the `Nonempty` wrapper can be

  at the headline `H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` (which does

- **M-3** — Inserted a new sub-lemma block

  - the forward map via `constantSheafAdj.homEquiv.symm` and the
    `simp [skyscraperPresheaf]` identification at `⊤`;
  - the inverse map pointwise on the two opens of PUnit (`⊥` via
    `IsTerminal.uniqueUpToIso`; `⊤` via the constantSheaf-unit-iso

  - naturality on `⊥ ≤ ⊤` via `Subsingleton` of terminal;
  - both Route A and Route B for the underlying Mathlib gap (the
    `Full`/`Faithful` instances on the one-point space).
  The parent `lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward` proof block has been
  updated to add `\uses{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}`
  and to explain the outer-vs-inner step decomposition that the Lean
  formalisation uses (replacing the pre-existing prose's silent
  presheaf-level argument).

  `lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (statement and proof) to
